result:
Build is clean (no errors). The task instructions say not to run the test program at the end — the mega-loop will re-test.

## Report

**Root cause:** `crates/lua-stdlib/src/loadlib.rs:455 readable()` was a stub that always returned `false`, and `crates/lua-stdlib/src/loadlib.rs:671 searcher_lua()` was a stub that always reported the load failed (`load_ok = false`). With both stubbed, the Lua-file searcher never recognized any file as existing, so `require "bwcoercion"` (and any module load) failed with an empty "not found" message.

**Fix:**
1. Added a `file_loader_hook: Option<FileLoaderHook>` to `GlobalState` (parallel to the existing `parser_hook`) since `std::fs` is banned in `lua-stdlib`.
2. `lua-cli/src/main.rs` now registers a hook that performs `std::fs::read` on the requested filename and propagates I/O errors as `LuaError::Runtime`.
3. Rewired `readable()` to probe via the hook and `searcher_lua()` to read the bytes via the hook and feed them to `state.load(...)` (with chunkname `@<filename>` to match C's convention).
4. `harness/run_official_test.sh` now exports `LUA_PATH` so `require` can find sibling modules (e.g. `bwcoercion.lua`) inside the testes directory.

**New state:** `cargo build -p lua-cli` is green (warnings only). Per the instructions I did not re-run the test program; the mega-loop will re-verify. Expectation: `require "bwcoercion"` now resolves the file via the hook, parses it, and execution proceeds past the require call in `bitwise.lua`.
